coredata.dc:title: Promoting Chlorella photosynthesis and bioresource production using directionally prepared carbon dots with tunable emission
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Ren
Fu, Liang
Dong, Shuangshi
Yang, Huiwen
Zhou, Dandan
coredata.dc:description: Green microalgae are promising and sustainable bioenergy and biomass feedstocks, that specifically utilize blue and red light for photosynthesis. Using carbon dots (CDs) to optimize photoluminescence is an attractive strategy for enhancing microalgal bioresource production; however, CD synthesis traditionally requires harsh conditions and laborious procedures. Little research has focused on developing CDs with tunable emission that precisely satisfy photosynthetic requirements. In this work, we directionally prepared non-toxic CDs using a simple method, which could adsorb light at spectra 500–600 nm and emit red light at 580–700 nm. CDs significantly promoted microalgae (Chlorella) growth by ~15%. Meanwhile, potential intracellular bioresources, pigments, carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids were generally enhanced. CDs, combined with an extracellular polymeric substance of microalgal cells, served as numerous micro-bulbs for Chlorella irradiation to sustainably provide optimized light. In this context, photosystems I and II were both stimulated. As such, we prepared CDs with tunable emission, which could significantly enhance microalgae and bioresource production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tunable emission
Photosynthesis
Microalgae
Growth
Bioresources

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Conversion and Storage and Environmental Technologies

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microalgae cultivation and carbon dot addition
Growth profile and specific growth rate
Prepared carbon dots with desired photoluminescence-tunable emission
The carbon dots driving Chlorella photosynthesis
Mechanisms of carbon dots driving photosynthesis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the prepared CDs. (a) TEM image and an inserted graph of nanoparticle size distributions; (b) HRTEM image of the CDs; (c) FFT pattern; (d) UV−Vis absorption and PL spectra; (e) FTIR spectrums; (f) XPS spectra for C1s; (h) XPS spectra for N1s; (h) toxicity analysis based on luminescent bacteria. All results were obtained based on experiments in triplicate and the error bars show standard deviations.

Effects of CD tunable emission on Chlorella growth profiles (a) and average growth rates at different stages (b). All results were obtained based on experiments in triplicate and the error bars show standard deviations. * represents significance P < 0.05, as compared with the control.

Effects of CDs tunable emissions on cell density-normalized pigment contents (a) and pigment productivities (b) at different CDs dosages. All results were obtained based on experiments in triplicate and the error bars show standard deviations. * represents significance P < 0.05, as compared with the control.

Effects of CD tunable emission on intracellular carbohydrate and protein contents (a and c), and carbohydrate and protein production and productivity (b and d). All results were obtained based on experiments in triplicate and the error bars show standard deviations. * represents significance P < 0.05, as compared with the control.

Effects of CD tunable emissions on lipid accumulation in microalgae cells. Lipid content (a), and production and productivity (b). All results were obtained based on experiments in triplicate and the error bars show standard deviations. * represents significance P < 0.05, as compared with the control.
The ultrastructure of Chlorella cells shown in TEM images for control (a) and CD protocols (b, 1 mg/L CDs; c, 5 mg/L CDs) on Day 4. p, pyrenoid; chl, chloroplast; v, vacuole; g, Golgi apparatus.

Optical microscopic observation in bright field (a, b) and fluorescent microscopy (c, d)—a and c were the same visual field, Chlorella without CDs; b and d were the same visual field, Chlorella with CD addition after 24 h. The excitation and emission wavelengths of fluorescent observation were 530–540 nm and 580–700 nm, respectively.

Effects of CDs on spectral properties of microalgae. XPS spectra for N1s (a) and C1s (b), and FTIR spectra (c). M, represents microalgae (control); C + M represents CD protocols with 1 mg/L CDs added to microalgae. The microalgal cells were collected on Day 6 and freeze-dried to a lyophilized powder for FTIR and XPS analysis.

Effects of CDs on PSII activity during microalgae cultivation (a) and light harvest complex gene expression on Day 4 (b). All results were obtained based on experiments in triplicate and the error bars show standard deviations. * represents significance P < 0.05, and ** represents significance P < 0.01, as compared with the control.
Primers for RT- PCR detection of light harvest complex genes.

row.entry: LHC I chlorophyll a/b binding protein 1
LHC I chlorophyll a/b binding protein 2
LHC I chlorophyll a/b binding protein 3
LHC I chlorophyll a/b binding protein 4
LHC I chlorophyll a/b binding protein 5

head.ce:title: Promoting Chlorella photosynthesis and bioresource production using directionally prepared carbon dots with tunable emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, Jilin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, Jilin, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Lab for Water Pollution Control and Resources Recovery, School of Environment, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130117, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Lab for Water Pollution Control and Resources Recovery

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Lab for Water Pollution Control and Resources Recovery, School of Environment, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130117, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green microalgae are promising and sustainable bioenergy and biomass feedstocks, that specifically utilize blue and red light for photosynthesis. Using carbon dots (CDs) to optimize photoluminescence is an attractive strategy for enhancing microalgal bioresource production; however, CD synthesis traditionally requires harsh conditions and laborious procedures. Little research has focused on developing CDs with tunable emission that precisely satisfy photosynthetic requirements. In this work, we directionally prepared non-toxic CDs using a simple method, which could adsorb light at spectra 500–600 nm and emit red light at 580–700 nm. CDs significantly promoted microalgae (Chlorella) growth by ~15%. Meanwhile, potential intracellular bioresources, pigments, carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids were generally enhanced. CDs, combined with an extracellular polymeric substance of microalgal cells, served as numerous micro-bulbs for Chlorella irradiation to sustainably provide optimized light. In this context, photosystems I and II were both stimulated. As such, we prepared CDs with tunable emission, which could significantly enhance microalgae and bioresource production.

ce:section.ce:para: Microalgae, with high photon conversion efficiency, are prolific and sustainable bioenergy and biomass feedstocks [1]. They can accumulate up to 50% high-quality lipid [2], and can generate diverse, high-value intracellular products, such as proteins, carbohydrates, specific fatty acids, and pigments [3].

Microalgae absorb light energy via chlorophyll and carotenoids to produce adenosine-triphosphate (ATP) for cellular activities. For green microalgae, chlorophyll a and b are the major light-harvesting pigments, which have dual-absorbance ranges of blue (450–480 nm) and red (605–700 nm) light [4]. In practice, sunlight or a light emitting diode (LED) are commonly employed for microalgae cultivation, due to their economic and light intensity advantages [5]. Sunlight is widely used for outdoor, open-pond systems, and LEDs for artificial photobioreactors [6]. However, the maximum value for the microalgae photosynthetic system to cover white light is typically no more than 10% [4]. Light with wavelengths at 500–600 nm are not well absorbed by microalgae, and light intensity in the dual-absorbance ranges (blue and red light) may be insufficient due to cell shading [7].

In this context, irradiating microalgae with specific red and blue light has been developed for increasing cell growth rate and improving lipid productivity [8]; however, the technique often is not economically practical. Nanomaterials, such as TiO2, ZnO, and quantum dots, have been shown to stimulate algae growth by spectral conversion of light, but they exert an ecological risk due to aquatic organism accumulation [9].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of fluorescent nanomaterial, sized below 10 nm, that have inspired research due to high quantum yield, chemical stability, superior biocompatibility, abundant sources, and low cost [10]. CDs possess outstanding tunable optical properties, and are commonly applied in biomedical fields, such as bioimaging and biosensing [11]. Using CDs to promote growth and photosynthesis of plants and algae is an attractive area of research [12]. However, CD synthetic approaches can be harsh and laborious, unavoidably employing electrochemical or plasma treatments, and applying microwave assist, reverse micelle, and pyrolytic processes [10]. More significantly, little research has focused on targeted design of CDs for shifting light wavelengths to the spectra that are precisely suitable for microalgae photosynthesis.

In this work, we synthesized CDs using a simple procedure under relative mild conditions. The CDs with tunable emission could directionally shift un-utilized light at wavelengths of 500–600 nm to red light to promote microalgae photosynthesis. The CDs adsorbed on the surface of the cells, sustainably providing optimized irradiation for biomass production. We elaborated characterization of CDs, specifically their contributions to improving biomass production, intracellular proteins, and carbohydrate and bioenergy generation. The mechanisms underlying CDs promotions were revealed by photosynthetic activity and function enzyme analysis. Applying the new synthesized CDs for microalgae culture is an efficient and economical strategy that holds much promise.

CD particles were prepared using a simple procedure with citric acid and formamide as precursors. Formamide was used as the reaction solvent and nitrogen source for CDs, the amine reacted with the carboxyl and formed graphitic N doped into the carbon core, which facilitated the long-wavelength emission of CDs [13,14]. Specifically, 50 mL 0.056 g/mL citric acid/formamide solution was treated at 160 °C for 3 h, and then 120 °C for another 3 h in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (Xiniu Tech, China). Then, 200 mL acetone was added to the solution after the mixture was cooled to room temperature. Dark precipitation was formed by storage at −20 °C for 12 h. The obtained precipitate was washed three times with acetone and methanol/acetone solution (10/90, v/v), respectively. Finally, the collection was dried at 50 °C, and CD products were obtained. CDs were dispersed in deionized water (sterility) to achieve a 250-mg CD/L storage solution. Before use, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove aggregates.

The model microalgae, Chlorella regularis (FACHB-729), was purchased from the Freshwater Algae Culture Collection Center of Institute of Hydrobiology in Wuhan, China. C. regularis was stored on BG11 agar jelly to at −20 °C. Before using, the cells were rejuvenated in BG11 liquid medium at 25 ± 1 °C for 48 h in a light incubator (IS-6L, Slientshake, China) with photoperiod 16 h/8 h (light/dark).
The BG11 medium was purchased from Hopebiol Co., Ltd. And all other reagents employed were of analytical grade and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microalgae cultivation and carbon dot addition
ce:section.ce:para: C. regularis at the exponential phase was centrifuged at 4,000 rpm at 4 °C for 10 min, and then inoculated to sterile BG11 medium at initial cell density of 3 × 106 cells mL−1. The microalgae cells were cultivated in a light incubator (IS-6L, Slientshake, China) with conditions of: temperature 25 °C, agitation 150 rpm, light intensity 4,000 Lux, photoperiod 16 h/8 h (light/dark). The LED light spectrum is shown in the Supplementary Material . CDs were added to the microalgae culture on Day 0, and Day 3 and Day 6, with dosages of 1 mg/L and 5 mg/L, respectively. Meanwhile, a no CD addition protocol was operated, under the same conditions, as a control treatment. All experiments were conducted in triplicate and error bars show standard deviations of three parallel samples. Results were repeated two times for all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth profile and specific growth rate
ce:section.ce:para: Chlorella growth profiles were obtained based on measurements of cell density (1 × 106 cell/mL) over time. Cell density was determined using a hemocytometer coupled with a microscope (Olympus, BX53, Japan). All experiments were run three times.

The specific growth rate (
μ
d−1) was calculated using the following formula [15], where N1 is the cell density at time t1, and N0 is the cell density at initial time, t0:
μ =
ln
N 1
- l n
N 0
t 1
-
t 0

Maximum quantum yield (Fv/Fm) was used to evaluate photosynthetic activity of photosystem II. We employed a Chlorophyll Fluorometer (AP110-C, AquaPen, Brno, Czech Republic) to measure the maximum fluorescence (Fm) and the minimum fluorescence (F0) of the cells, via irradiation with saturated pulsed light. Fv is the difference between Fm and F0. The maximum quantum yield was calculated using the equation: Fv/Fm = (Fm − F0)/Fm [16].

Total chlorophyll and carotenoid amounts were measured according to previous studies [17]. Briefly, 5 mL of cell culture was centrifuged at 13,500 rpm at 4 °C for 5 min. The supernatant was discarded, and the precipitation was resuspended in 5 mL 90% methanol. The suspension was bathed at 60 °C for 10 min and centrifuged again. The supernatant was collected for measuring absorbance of the pigments using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2800A, UNICO Co. Ltd, China), at 470, 652, and 665 nm. The content of pigments was calculated as follows:
Chlorophyll a (mg/L) = 16.82 A665-9.28 A652
Chlorophyll b (mg/L) = 36.92 A652-16.54 A665
Carotenoid (mg/L) = (1000A470-1.19 Ca-95.15 Cb)/225
Where A470, A652, and A652 are the absorbances of supernatant at 470, 652, and 665 nm, respectively.

The cell density-normalized pigment content was determined by the ratio of pigment content and cell density. Pigment productivity was the ratio of the pigment content difference and the interval term of sampling.

The culture was centrifuged at 13,500 rpm, at 4 °C for 30 min, to remove extracellular polymers. Supernatant was discarded and the pellets were collected. The collected pellets were freeze-dried in a lyophilizer (Pilot1-2LD, Boyikang, China), at −60 °C for 2 days, to obtain dried algal powder.

For carbohydrate extraction, 10 mg of algal powder was suspended in 3 m L of 80% ethanol and incubated at 68 °C for 15 min. Then, the suspension was centrifuged (13,500 rpm), and the supernatant was retained and the precipitate was added to 1.5 mL 30% perchloric acid and mixed for 15 min. Finally, the two supernatants were combined and measured by the anthrone-sulfuric acid method to determine carbohydrate content [18].

Before protein measurement, it was extracted by the following steps: 10 mg of algal powder was pretreated by 80% ethanol for 3 h; the mixture was centrifuged (13,500 rpm) to remove the supernatant; and 2 mL of 2 mol/L NaOH was added to the deposit and incubated at 100 °C for 10 min. Then, protein content of supernatant was determined using the method of Bradford [19].
The productivity of carbohydrates/proteins was calculated compared to that of pigments.

The lipid content was determined by a modified-gravimetric method [20]. 3 mL chloroform/methanol (2/1, v/v) was added for lipid extraction of 30 mg algal powder, and they were mixed on a shaker (Mix-100, Allsheng, China) in the dark for 10 h. The mixture was centrifuged at 1,000 rpm to obtain supernatant, and then 1.5 mL of the above-described extraction reagent was added, followed by sonication at 40 kHz for 30 min. Samples were centrifuged again and then methanol and 0.1% NaCl solution were added to make a final ratio of methanol/chloroform/water of 1/1/0.9. Finally, the bottom layer was collected and dried at 40 °C using a nitrogen evaporator (KD 2000, Allsheng, China). Lipid productivity was calculated as in Section 2.5.2.

The morphology and crystal structure of prepared CDs were identified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, via a 200 kV FEI-Tecnai F20, USA) observation. The ultrastructure of algal cells and CD-treated algal samples were verified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 1200EX, JEOL, Japan). Before TEM observation, cells were treated with a series of fixation, dehydration, embedding, and sectioning according to our previous study [21].

The fluorescence of CD-treated algae was identified using a fluorescence microscope with excitation light of 530–540 nm (Olympus, BX53, Japan). Prior to operation, cells were centrifuged at 6,000 rpm and washed three times with 20 mM phosphate buffer solution.

CDs and microalgae samples were lyophilized (details are given above) before Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, CARY 630, Agilent Technologies Inc., USA) analysis and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG ESCALAB210, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) characterization. XPS analysis used an Al Ka (h = 1486.6 eV) radiation excitation source, and spectra are calibrated through the neutral C1s peak at 284.6 eV.
The UV–Vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were carried out by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer (F97, Lengguang Tech, China).

The toxicity of CDs was tested by the luminous inhibition rate of luminescent bacteria Q67 (purchased from Beijing Hammatsu Photo Techniques Inc., Beijing, China) [22]. The luminescence was measured with a luminometer (BHP9505, Hamamatsu, China) and CD concentrations were 0, 1, 5, 10, and 20 mg/L, respectively.

The real-time PCR primers of genes encoding photosynthesis antenna proteins were designed based on our previous work [23]; the gene and primer sequence 5′-3′ are given in Table 1 . The RNA extraction, cDNA cloning, and SYBR green RT-PCR experiments were developed according to a previous study [24]. The relative expression of genes was determined with the 2−(ΔΔCt) ratio. Microalgae samples were collected on Day 4 and RT-PCR analysis was conducted in triplicate.

To determine effects of CDs on microalgae growth, intracellular components, photosynthesis, and gene expressions, we compared differences between CDs protocols and controls. Statistical significance was analyzed with independent-sample t-tests. All analyses were conducted using the SPSS software package (IBM SPSS statistic 23.0) and P < 0.05 indicated significance [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prepared carbon dots with desired photoluminescence-tunable emission
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were prepared by the solvothermal method using citric acid and formamide as precursors. The TEM image in Fig. 1 a shows the monodispersed CDs. In the insets of Fig. 1a, the statistics of particle size distribution show that the size of the CDs was in the range 1.2–4.0 nm with an average size ~2.57 nm. The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image shows lattice spacing of CDs at 0.212 nm (Fig. 1b), which corresponded to the d-spacing of graphene (1 0 0) planes [26]. This could be verified by the fast Fourier transformed (FFT) pattern of HRTEM shown in Fig. 1c.

The optical properties of CDs were examined according to their UV–Vis absorption and PL excitation and emission spectra (Fig. 1d). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of prepared CDs displayed a wide and strong adsorption in the region of 300–800 nm with two major peaks centered at 350 nm and 520 nm. The former peak was attributed to the n–π* transition of CO and conjugated CN [27], while the latter to the typical absorption of an aromatic system with extended conjugation in the CD structure [28]. When exposed to the excitation wavelength in the adsorption spectrum (500–600 nm), the PL emission peaks possessed a red-shift (Fig. S3), with a strong and wide emission 580–700 nm and a peak ~625 nm (at 540 nm emission). Since emission wavelength varied with the excitation wavelength, the CDs had excitation a wavelength-dependent feature (tunable emission) [29]. Such tunable emission features were attributed to the quantum dimensions of size, and complex chemical groups of CDs [30]. The shifting of the spectrum intensified red light, and could promote green microalgae photosynthesis [31].

The FTIR spectrum is shown in Fig. 1e, and stretching vibrations of OH at 3470 cm−1, NH2 at 3100–3400 cm−1, and CH3 CH2 at 2846 and 2965 cm−1 were observed [32]. The sharp peak at 1645 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of CN/CO groups, the peak at 1348 cm−1 to the bending vibration of sp3 CH [33], and the peaks from 1280 to 1100 cm−1 to the stretching vibrations of COC [34]. Aromatic CH was represented by the adsorption peak at ~800 cm−1 [35].

Fig. 1f and g show the XPS survey of CDs, which demonstrate the existence of elemental composition (C1s and N1s). Three diffraction peaks for C1s at 284.6, 285.3, and 288.4 eV were attributed to the CC bond with sp2 orbital, CN, and CN bonds, respectively [36]. Three types of N, including pyridinic N (399.4 eV), pyrrolic N (399.8 eV), and graphitic N (400.7 eV) can be observed from the N1s spectrum in Fig. 1g [27,30,37]. Peaks of O1s at 531.1 eV and 532.2 eV were assigned to CO and CO (Fig. S2), respectively, corresponding to the XPS results for O1s [38]. All results confirmed the graphite-like core and the surface-rich functional groups with N and O constituting the CDs.

The toxicity of CDs was tested by standard toxicology methods performed on luminescent bacteria Q67 (Fig. 1h). CDs had no cytotoxicity at low concentrations (≤5 mg/L); the slight promotion of cell viability probably was caused by hormesis effects of CDs. The bacteria illuminance was inhibited by 3%–8% when CD concentrations were greater than 10 mg/L. We employed a CD dosage of less than 5 mg/L, due to its bio-toxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The carbon dots driving Chlorella photosynthesis

ce:section.ce:para: Effects of CDs on Chlorella growth were verified by cell densities shifts with cultivation time, as shown in Fig. 2 . The synthesized CDs emitted more intensified red light by shifting photoluminescence, contributing to greater cell density over the control when the CD dosage was 1 mg/L. Furthermore, such enhancement was more obvious since the cell density reached ~ 1.5 × 107, and the cells too concentrated to allow light to penetrate uniformly. CDs served as numerous micro-bulbs, which illuminated Chlorella to provide optimized light energy. The control without CDs entered stationary stage at least 2 days earlier than with 1 mg/L CDs. Overall, the cell density for CDs at 1 mg/L increased by ~ 15%.

However, the dosage of 5 mg/L CDs only caused a slight Chlorella growth enhancement (1.3%). This probably was because CDs converted too much illuminant intensity at the specified wavelength range (580–700 nm), which damaged the repair mechanism of photosystem II, and led to the inactivation of downstream oxygen-evolving enzymes and electron carriers [39].

Pigments are high value chemical compounds produced by microalgae. The content and production of pigments are quantified in µg/106 cell and in mg/L/d, respectively, to report effects of CDs, as shown in Fig. 3 . There was a clear trend that 1 mg/L CDs could improve pigment accumulation in Chlorella cells. Contents of chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, and carotenoid increased ~ 8% at the exponential phase (Day 8) and 5% at the early stationary phase (Day 10–12). Accordingly, the productivity was 29% and 24% higher at those phases, respectively. In addition to violet blue, both chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b adsorb red light at wavelengths of 600–700 nm—exactly the emitting range of synthesized CDs. Thus, CDs significantly promoted the production of chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b (P < 0.05), and, in turn, they transferred light energy to the reaction center of photosystems to improve cell growth, as mentioned above. Carotenoid production also increased, because biosynthesis was coordinated with biogenesis of chlorophylls and proteins of the photosynthetic apparatus, as well as with electron transport [40].

Consistent with the above growth profiles, 5 mg/L CD dosages caused loss of multiple pigments. Photosynthetic pigments are basic elements that absorb and transmit light energy, however, the high intensity of the CD tunable light leads to photooxidation of pigments [41].

Chlorella is a reliable, rich source of vegetable carbohydrates and proteins, which are high-value products for pharmaceutical, bio-plastic, and food industries [3]. The effects of CDs on the content and production of carbohydrates and protein are shown in Fig. 4 . Again, protein and carbohydrate contents were promoted 6% and 8% by 1 mg/L CDs, respectively. Combining the improved cell growth, the corresponding productivities increased 21% and 30%, respectively, which were both statistically significant (P < 0.05).

Effects of CDs on lipid content and production are shown in Fig. 5 . CDs had negligible effects on lipid content, probably because microalgae cultivation had not reached the starvation phase and thus lipids had not accumulated [2]. It is noteworthy that lipid productivity and production significantly increased by 15% and 10%, respectively, at Day 6 for the CD 1 mg/L protocol (P < 0.05), attributed to significantly enhanced biomass. They reached as much as 18 mg/L/d and 259 mg/L at Day 12. The above results implied CDs enhanced microalga lipid production with shorter cultivation times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of carbon dots driving photosynthesis

ce:section.ce:para: TEM images show effects of CD tunable emission on microalgal morphology and intracellular structure (Fig. 6 ). Compared to untreated cells, the 1 mg/L CD treatment did not toxify Chlorella. The intracellular microbial organelles, such as pyrenoids, chloroplasts, vacuoles, and the Golgi apparatus all retained integrity. However, with 5 mg/L CD dosage, cells presented photooxidation symptoms, chloroplasts were blurry, and vacuoles were present, caused by the loss of intracellular substances.

The art statuses of CDs in the microalgae culture were revealed by a series of microscopic observations and spectrum analyses. Microalgae had no obvious differences with and without CD addition in bright field images observed by optical microscopy (Fig. 7 a and b). According to the fluorescent microscopic image, CDs were attached on the microalgae cell surfaces (Fig. 7c and d), and stably served as optimized light energy supplies. XPS spectra also provides evidence for CDs combining with algae, as shown in Fig. 8 a and b. The unique peaks at 285.4 eV (attributed to CO/CN) and 400.7 eV (associated with graphitic N), which both belong to CD nanoparticles, only appeared for CDs treated with Chlorella samples. However, XPS spectra of O1s for microalgae had no obvious differences compared to microalgae with CD addition (Fig. S4).

FTIR spectra reveal bindings of CDs with Chlorella cells were generally based on functional groups of polysaccharides and proteins, as shown in Fig. 8c. A number of functional groups marked composition of extracellular polymeric substances (EPSs), observed at the banding range 400–4000 cm−1 [42]. Some changes were elucidated when the cells combined with CDs. Bands at 831–843 cm−1 belonged to carbohydrates, whereas the sharper splitting peaks at 1024–1066 cm−1 were assigned to CO stretching of polysaccharides [42]. The vibration of the FTIR band at 557–587 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the CNS group of the amine peak; the decreased intensity of the spectrum in the region of 1570–1575 cm−1 was attributed to amide II [42]. They all confirmed a role of proteins in CD bindings. Such changes suggest CDs interacted with Chlorella EPSs, and that CDs could emit light-energy to microalgae cells.

Synthesis of ATP and of reducing power (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate, NADPH) for fixing carbon to support Chlorella biosynthesis depended on electron transfer between photosystems II and I (PSII and PSI) [43]. PSII activity was represented by Fv/Fm, and PSI activity was verified by functional enzyme expressions, as shown in Fig. 9 a. Fv/Fm was 0.60 initially for the control when there was no CD stimulation, and slightly decreased at the stationary phase (Day 12). PSII activity significantly improved (P < 0.05) when CDs served for photoluminescence tunable emission. This photosynthetic activity enhancement was attributed to a more efficient light capture, and light wavelength distributions were optimized by CDs (light shifted 500–600 nm to 580–700 nm). Subsequently, unidirectional excitation energy could be transferred to thechlorophyll-pigmentbed ofPSII reactioncenters [43].

PCR results show the functional enzymes in the PSI reaction center were generally up-regulated when the light wavelength was shifted by CDs. Chlorophyll a and b binding protein-encoding genes (LHCA1, LHCA2, LHCA3, LHCA4 and LHCA5) were significantly up-regulated (Fig. 9b). These photosynthesis antenna proteins control the light-harvesting complex associated with PSI. Antenna proteins absorbed the photons and exited chlorophyll molecules, and the energy was transferred to reaction centers and activated the photochemical reactions [44].

In this study, we directionally prepared no cytotoxic carbon dots with tunable emission using a simple and economic method under mild conditions (instead of traditional harsh conditions and laborious procedures) [10], and then creatively use them in microalgae cultivation. The CDs could specifically shift the light spectrum at 500–600 nm to red light (580–700 nm). This promoted microalgae growth by 15%. Intracellular pigments, carbohydrates, proteins, and lipid production were significantly enhanced. CDs adsorbed on the surface of microalgal cells via extracellular polysaccharides and proteins, and sustainably provide optimized irradiation for microalgae. In this way, the photosynthetic activity was stimulated, and the light harvest complex gene expression up-regulated. CDs applied to microalgae cultivation is an efficient and economical strategy to obtain more biomass, bioenergy, and improve intracellular components, and has better performance than other light conversion materials, such as metal nanoparticles and photosensitizers [45,46]. This study provide a new clue of CDs potential in microalgae industry, however, the fate of CDs in microalgal cells and the possible long-term effects should be also evaluated in further study.

Ren Xue: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Liang Fu: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Shuangshi Dong: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Huiwen Yang: Validation, Investigation. Dandan Zhou: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51578117, 51722803, 51708095).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.02.080.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
